chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue - FinTech Global
articles.snippet: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue  FinTech Global

articles.title: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure - SDxCentral
articles.snippet: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure  SDxCentral

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

post1.text:
Most companies scaling past $50M don't have a strategy problem. They have a leadership bench problem.

When companies like Ciena make deliberate executive appointments to support a major infrastructure push, they're not reacting to growth. They're engineering it. That distinction matters enormously.

Here's what leaders at scaling companies consistently get wrong:

1. They promote their best operators into leadership roles without asking whether those people are actually built to lead at the next level of complexity. Loyalty is not the same as capability.

2. They wait until a gap is painful before filling it. By then, the cost isn't just a vacancy. It's the decisions that didn't get made and the momentum that quietly stalled.

3. They treat executive hiring as an HR function instead of a growth investment. Who sits at your leadership table is a strategic decision, not an administrative one.

Scaling is not a revenue milestone. It's an organizational stress test.

So here's the question worth sitting with: Is your current leadership team built for the company you have, or the company you're building?

#Leadership #Scaling #BusinessGrowth

post2.text:
A recent piece in SDxCentral covered how Ciena just added key executives across supply chain and product to support their AI infrastructure scaling goals. It's a smart move, and the reasoning behind it applies well beyond the tech sector.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. The leaders who scale successfully don't wait for their org chart to buckle under pressure. They build the team ahead of the growth curve, not after it.

Adding executive capacity in targeted functional areas before demand peaks is one of the highest-leverage moves a scaling company can make. The cost of the wrong person in the wrong seat at the wrong time far exceeds the cost of a well-timed hire.

If your leadership structure was built for the company you were two years ago, it's worth a hard look now.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixwFBVV95cUxOQm05NW1RSUh0Uk1fa0hYMU9QTHVnaXJhZEpfdVVjaE9uWURQS3QtNGxCMWZhWjNwdWUyeXFwengxbDZZRFd6OF91ZTdtUmtNSGoyTkRzdklfeENYQ09ITWN6UjhWQVE0UE5sQmx3MXZJdmNjLVhSM2plUXl5aXhyOS1aS2hCb2xYQVU4UUdDakphV3NPWTdfazVQVWJtUEs2bFJWY3VzeTk5N2tVN3dFdE5TOFlqMUZxaWZvdTRfQ2w2QmVOUFF3?oc=5

post3.text:
When Buffini and Company recently brought on three senior executives to scale their coaching operations, they made a leadership bet. That kind of bet is exactly what Chapter 1 of our upcoming book addresses head-on.

Chapter 1 is called "Lead Like Lives Depend On It," written by Katherine Slain. The title is not a metaphor.

Katherine's core argument is that leadership at the scaling stage carries real consequences that most operators underestimate. The decisions you make about who leads, how they lead, and whether your leadership model can absorb the pressure of rapid growth will determine whether you break through or break down.

One insight she surfaces early: the leadership behaviors that got your company to $50M are often the exact behaviors that will slow you down on the road to $500M.

There's a framework in this chapter that changes how you think about your own seat at the table.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Companies don't stall on the way to $500M because they run out of customers. They stall because they run out of leadership capacity. The executives who built a $50M business are not automatically the right people to scale it to ten times that size. Understanding the gap between those two versions of your leadership team is where the real work begins.
posts.email_subject: Your $50M team can't take you to $500M

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs - J.P. Morgan
articles.snippet: Business Exit Strategies for Startup Founders & Entrepreneurs  J.P. Morgan

articles.title: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now - inc.com
articles.snippet: Why Boomer Business Owners Need to Set Exit Strategies Now  inc.com

post1.text:
Most business owners think about their exit too late. The ones who win think about it from day one.

Your exit multiple is not determined at the negotiation table. It is built over years through decisions that either compress or expand what a buyer will pay. By the time you hire a banker, the score is mostly set.

Inc. recently highlighted how an entire generation of business owners is approaching exit windows without adequate preparation. The gap between what they expect and what the market will pay is often staggering.

Here is what mid-market leaders at the $50M to $500M range need to understand:

1. Revenue quality matters more than revenue size. Recurring, predictable revenue commands a premium. Lumpy, customer-concentrated revenue gets discounted hard.

2. EBITDA adjustments buyers accept depend on how clean your books are today, not at close.

3. Management depth is a multiplier. If you are the business, buyers price in the risk of losing you.

So here is the real question: Are you running your company to operate it, or to maximize what someone will pay for it one day?

post2.text:
A recent piece from the U.S. Chamber of Commerce walks through the fundamentals of building a business exit strategy, covering options like mergers, acquisitions, family succession, and liquidation.

It is solid foundational content. But here is what it does not address: for companies scaling through the $50M to $500M range, the exit conversation is not a planning exercise. It is a value creation discipline.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who realize too late that operational decisions made two or three years before a transaction directly shaped their multiple. Buyer scrutiny at this level is intense. Preparation is not a checklist. It is a strategy.

If you are leading a company in this range and have not stress-tested your valuation assumptions, now is the time.

Read the Chamber piece here as a starting point: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMihAFBVV95cUxONERpMjFwUjkzVW8xLTRScUhVWTQ5VUFMS1R5cFY4c2p2eXYzZ3NYc0V0NUlaMmREUXM2N3VSaThYUjhERGNiSmhFTDB5TVBYNEhrOU9WSHRyYThWWUlUY2RYNlVFNkhvVGU0V3h1bXAtVzUwVVh0bG5FOWxmTUNJTTBJYjI?oc=5

post3.text:
J.P. Morgan recently published a breakdown of exit strategies for founders and entrepreneurs, and it reinforces something we hear from leaders at scaling companies constantly: they know an exit is coming, but they are not building toward one.

Chapter 2 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit. Chapter authors Paul Belair and George Hohman work with mid-market companies every day on exactly this challenge.

One thing they make clear in the chapter: the levers that move your valuation multiple are not financial engineering moves made at the end. They are operational and structural decisions made years before any deal gets done.

If you are leading a $50M to $500M company, this chapter will change how you think about the decisions you are making right now.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most mid-market business owners believe their exit strategy is a future problem. It is not. Every pricing decision, customer contract, and leadership hire you make today is either building or eroding your valuation multiple. By the time a buyer is in the room, the number is largely already written. Here is what the $50M to $500M range demands you understand before that moment arrives.
posts.email_subject: Your exit multiple is set before you sell

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Wrike Now Offers a Strategic Portfolio Management Solution to Bridge Strategy and Execution, Enabling Organizations to Plan and Deliver True Transformation  Business Wire

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

articles.title: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It - Forbes
articles.snippet: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It  Forbes

post1.text:
Most strategic plans fail not because the strategy was wrong, but because execution was treated as an afterthought.

Forbes recently examined why leadership teams consistently collapse at the execution stage. The finding is uncomfortable: the plan itself is rarely the problem. The problem is that leaders confuse creating a strategy with running a business.

Here is what $50M+ leaders need to hear:

1. A strategy that lives in a deck is not a strategy. It is a wish. Execution requires ownership at every level, not just the executive suite.

2. The gap between planning and performance widens when accountability is ambiguous. Someone must own each initiative, with a deadline and a measurable outcome attached.

3. Revisiting your plan quarterly is not optional. Markets shift. Your plan should too.

Scaling past $100M demands that strategy and operations breathe together, not exist in separate rooms.

So here is the real question: When did your leadership team last stress-test whether your strategy is actually being executed, or just being reported on?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes breaks down exactly why leadership teams fall apart when it comes to executing their own strategies. Worth a read for any operator trying to scale.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $200M range. Leadership builds a solid strategic plan, then hands it off and assumes momentum carries it forward. It does not.

The fix is not a better planning template. It is building an execution culture where priorities are visible, owners are named, and progress is reviewed on a cadence that actually holds people accountable.

Strategy without execution infrastructure is just expensive guesswork.

If your team is strong on vision but struggling to close the gap between plan and performance, start by asking who specifically owns each strategic priority today.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxOSXpNczJQdVBKTWlEV0dsby1FaEFGUVg5X1B3Zk9qUGI2bDJMSUxydGhRdURFUUdERXhBRmNzUndTVXhITTREMzMxdk9faE5rUldabk9ld2p2dHdfeXJjVEpMckJ6dFZNckFkTkk5ZmJ5MHRVSm5iSXo2WGlwc0NoeTBvcUYxTF9pbUNmaDB4LUdlNk9pMll0Y01BRTNVYzJObzRrV3pCN19scHYtdThYcHJVUVFYbjFMbkE

post3.text:
The conversation around strategy execution is loud right now, and for good reason. Most companies plan well and execute poorly.

In Chapter 3 of our upcoming book Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," authors Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall share what they discovered building and scaling businesses in industries that could not be more different from each other.

The through line? Execution discipline looks the same regardless of what you sell. The companies that scaled were not the ones with the smartest strategies. They were the ones that built systems to make sure the strategy actually happened, every quarter, at every level.

There is one insight in this chapter that reframes how leadership teams should think about accountability that we have not seen articulated this clearly anywhere else.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leadership team has a strategy. Almost none of them have a real execution system. The gap between those two things is where growth goes to stall. Companies scaling past $50M cannot afford to treat execution as a downstream problem. It is the core problem. Here is what high-performing leadership teams do differently when strategy and performance finally have to meet.
posts.email_subject: Your strategy isn't the problem. This is.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: How to Double B2B Sales Growth - Bain & Company
articles.snippet: How to Double B2B Sales Growth  Bain & Company

post1.text:
Most companies at $50M+ don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. A habit is what your team does because it worked three years ago. A strategy is a deliberate, documented plan for how you win, who you win with, and what you stop doing to make room for growth.

Bain & Company recently found that doubling B2B sales growth comes down to a handful of specific, repeatable decisions, not harder work or bigger headcount. The companies that scale past $100M aren't outworking anyone. They're outthinking their own past.

Three things leaders at this stage consistently get wrong:

1. Confusing revenue activity with revenue strategy
2. Letting the top rep define the process instead of the other way around
3. Treating pipeline reviews as accountability theater instead of strategic diagnostics

If your sales results depend on who shows up that day, you don't have a strategy.

What would change in your business if your worst sales month looked like your best one?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Bain & Company breaks down what actually separates B2B companies that double their sales growth from those that plateau. The findings are worth a hard look if you're leading a company in the $50M to $500M range.

The throughline is this: growth at scale is not a selling problem. It is a strategy and structure problem.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. Companies with strong products and capable sales teams still stall because no one has defined the right customers, the right motion, or the right metrics to manage toward.

If your revenue feels unpredictable despite a full pipeline, the issue is almost never effort. It is almost always clarity.

Read the Bain piece and ask your leadership team what your answer is to the same question.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMieEFVX3lxTE9KUjR4bFFkR0FPV0NzcF96cEVjVExiZDhMNFVSTHZfYk10UEtuYW8xVmh5blI5a2VSck4ySjhQTTZpQzNBQ3JRWVE3VjhjcEx3SVM2LUpQRmJELUJPS3psdExNdVRKaUpGYVJ6dmM4M3l2blJCSHAybA

post3.text:
Bain & Company recently published research on what it actually takes to double B2B sales growth. The answer points directly at something our chapter authors Deb and Brandon address head-on in Chapter 4 of Rock Your Business.

The chapter is titled "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation." That title is not an exaggeration.

Deb and Brandon work with scaling companies every week and they see the same pattern: leaders assume that because revenue is growing, a strategy exists. Those are not the same thing. One insight they surface early in the chapter is the cost of ambiguity in your sales motion, not the visible cost, but the compounding cost no one is measuring.

If your team is busy but your growth is inconsistent, this chapter was written for you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: At $50M in revenue, most companies believe they have a sales strategy. What they actually have is a collection of habits that got them here. Bain & Company research confirms it: doubling B2B growth requires deliberate structure, not more effort. Here is what scaling companies are getting wrong, and what the ones breaking through are doing differently.
posts.email_subject: Your sales habit isn't a strategy

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

articles.title: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez - PRWeek
articles.snippet: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez  PRWeek

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is the feeling people get when they walk away from any interaction with your company.

JBL figured this out. Instead of chasing campaign after campaign, they built their marketing around culture. The result is a brand that people feel rather than simply recognize. That is a completely different game.

For leaders scaling past $50M, here is what that means in practice:

First, your brand has a smell. Customers, employees, and prospects sense it before they can name it. Consistency in how you treat people, communicate, and deliver is what creates that scent.

Second, campaigns are tactics. Culture is strategy. If your marketing stops the moment the campaign ends, you have a tactics problem, not a marketing department.

Third, brand alignment is an internal job before it is an external one. Your team has to live it before your market can feel it.

So here is the real question: If your company disappeared tomorrow, would anyone actually notice the absence of something they loved?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators on JBL's marketing approach caught our attention. JBL is deliberately shifting away from one-off campaigns and building their marketing around culture as the core engine.

For companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, this distinction is critical and often missed. At that growth stage, campaigns are easy to fund but hard to sustain. Culture, on the other hand, compounds. It shapes how every employee shows up, how every customer is treated, and how your brand is remembered when no ad is running.

The companies that scale well do not just market more. They build something people want to stay close to.

If your marketing only works when you are spending, it is time to look at what your culture is actually communicating. Read the full piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
JBL building their marketing around culture rather than campaigns is not a trend. It is a principle that scaling companies have to internalize if they want growth that lasts.

Chapter 5 of Rock Your Business, titled 'What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?', tackles exactly this. Written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West, the chapter opens with a question most leaders have never thought to ask: what invisible signal is your brand sending before anyone reads a word you have written?

The insight they share is that by the time a $50M company is pushing toward $500M, brand drift is already happening. Most leaders do not see it until customers start feeling it.

There is a framework inside this chapter that helps you diagnose that drift before it costs you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most growing companies treat marketing as output: ads, content, campaigns, spend. But the brands that scale without breaking have figured out something quieter and harder to copy. Their marketing is a byproduct of how they actually operate. Before you approve next quarter's budget, it is worth asking one uncomfortable question: what does your company smell like to someone who has never heard of you?
posts.email_subject: What does your brand actually smell like?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring - hrtoday.in
articles.snippet: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring  hrtoday.in

articles.title: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner  Business Wire

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

post1.text:
Hiring more people is not a growth strategy. It is a cost problem dressed up as ambition.

SHRM recently reported that the new standard for hiring in 2026 is precision over volume. Companies that scaled headcount aggressively are now quietly right-sizing, while leaner teams with sharper hiring criteria are outperforming them.

Here is what that means for leaders running $50M+ businesses:

1. Your next hire should solve a specific, documented constraint. If you cannot name the bottleneck, you are not ready to hire for it.

2. Speed-to-productivity matters more than speed-to-hire. A role filled in 30 days that takes 9 months to perform is not a win.

3. Talent strategy is a business model decision. Who you hire, and when, determines which markets you can actually serve.

The companies scaling toward $500M are not building the biggest teams. They are building the most deliberate ones.

What is the last hire your company made that you would make again without hesitation?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM made the case that precision hiring is replacing high-volume recruiting as the dominant model heading into 2026. Worth reading for any leadership team that has felt the pain of a fast hire gone wrong.

At Autobahn Consultants, we see this constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. Growth pressure creates urgency, urgency creates shortcuts, and shortcuts create misaligned teams that slow the very growth you were trying to accelerate.

The fix is not a better job posting. It is a talent strategy that is built into your operating plan before the seat is even open.

If your hiring process is reactive, your growth will be too. Read the article and ask your leadership team: are we hiring precisely or just quickly?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
SHRM's recent reporting on precision hiring is not a trend. It is a signal that the rules of building teams at scale have permanently changed.

Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business, titled The Talent Engine, addresses exactly this. Authors Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West worked with companies across the $50M to $500M growth range and found one consistent gap: leaders were making hiring decisions based on organizational charts that reflected where the company was, not where it was going.

The chapter introduces a framework for thinking about talent as an engine, not a roster. One insight that stops leaders cold: the hire that feels urgent today is often the one that creates the bottleneck tomorrow.

If you are building a team to carry your company through its next stage of growth, this chapter is required reading.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling companies treat hiring as a response to growth. The ones that actually reach $500M treat it as a driver of growth. There is a meaningful difference between those two postures, and it shows up in your org chart, your margins, and your momentum. Here is what precision talent strategy actually looks like when the pressure is on.
posts.email_subject: Your hiring process is slowing you down

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
Most growing companies don't suffer from a lack of data. They suffer from too much of it.

Once a business crosses $50M, the instinct is to measure everything. Every department adds its own dashboard. Every leadership meeting gets longer. And somewhere in all that noise, the decisions that actually move the business get slower, not faster.

Oracle NetSuite recently outlined 30 financial metrics worth tracking heading into 2026. Thirty. For most leadership teams, that list would create paralysis, not clarity.

Here's what high-performing scaling companies do differently:

1. They pick 5 to 7 metrics that directly connect to their strategic priorities, and they defend that short list aggressively.
2. They separate lag indicators from lead indicators. Knowing what happened last quarter is not the same as knowing where the business is heading.
3. They assign a single owner to each metric. Shared accountability is no accountability.

The companies that scale well don't measure more. They measure what matters and act on it fast.

What's one metric your leadership team tracks that almost never changes a decision?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Business.com rounded up 14 tools designed to help businesses track KPIs more effectively. The options are solid, and the comparisons are useful if you're evaluating your current stack.

But here's the angle most scaling companies miss: the tool is rarely the problem.

When a company between $50M and $200M struggles with KPI visibility, it's almost never because they chose the wrong software. It's because the metrics themselves weren't defined clearly before the tool was selected. You can't automate your way out of a measurement strategy problem.

Before you evaluate another platform, get your leadership team aligned on which 5 to 7 numbers genuinely predict business performance. Then find the tool that surfaces those numbers clearly.

The article is worth a read as a starting point: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMingFBVV95cUxQcWNPSTVTUDVYYURoVkZKa0ItNEtQbmJqWlNJTlIzUVZqclAxMHFaajNobC1JbGNkZGZVajN6MWJLT1pJcXktVG10VG8zUUljdU43NU5WR1cyNDB4QkxONlJkb3hJR2FfNzdGZHd1VWhPbTktdU1vdTEzamFWY2ZFVDNKUFIwRG91ZDdWU1UyN2twNFpLd1JjU3EzTUpjdw

post3.text:
CLA recently published guidance on the KPIs manufacturers should use to improve company performance. It's a practical list, and it reflects something we see constantly in scaling businesses: leaders know they need better metrics, but most don't have a framework for knowing when their KPIs have actually become a liability.

Chapter 7 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, tackles this directly. Titled 'Death by KPIs,' the chapter was authored by Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, and it confronts a problem most executives won't admit out loud: at a certain stage of growth, more measurement creates more confusion, not more control.

One insight they surface early: the number of KPIs on your leadership dashboard is often inversely related to how well your team understands the business.

That tension is worth sitting with before your next planning cycle.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: At some point between $50M and $200M, your KPIs stop driving performance and start consuming it. Leadership meetings balloon. Dashboards multiply. And the metrics meant to create accountability quietly become a substitute for actual decisions. This is the trap Chapter 7 of Rock Your Business was written to help you avoid. Here's what scaling companies get wrong about measurement, and what the best ones do instead.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs slowing you down?

articles.title: ALX Enterprise Bets on Executive AI Readiness as Firms Race to Future-Proof Workforces - The Kenyan Wallstreet
articles.snippet: ALX Enterprise Bets on Executive AI Readiness as Firms Race to Future-Proof Workforces  The Kenyan Wallstreet

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

post1.text:
The executives winning with AI right now are not the ones using the most tools. They are the ones who decided what AI is actually for inside their organization.

Harvard Business Review recently made the case that traditional performance metrics no longer capture what matters in an AI-augmented workplace. That is not a footnote. That is a signal that your entire operating model may be measuring the wrong things.

Here is what that means for leaders scaling past $50M:

First, if your KPIs were built before AI entered your workflows, they are probably rewarding effort instead of outcomes. Second, the companies pulling ahead are not just deploying AI tools, they are rebuilding how they define and measure productive work. Third, AI readiness is not an IT initiative. It is a leadership decision that belongs in the boardroom.

Most companies treat AI adoption as a technology upgrade. The ones scaling fast treat it as an organizational redesign.

What metric in your business would you eliminate first if AI made it obsolete?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times highlighted a growing trend: busy executives are deploying AI versions of themselves to handle meetings, answer questions, and communicate on their behalf.

Read that again.

For companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, this is not a novelty story. It is a warning about where leadership bandwidth breaks down at scale. The bottleneck in most growing companies is not capital or talent. It is executive time and judgment, and AI is starting to stand in the gap.

At Autobahn, we see this play out in every engagement. The leaders who scale well are the ones who ruthlessly protect their highest-value decisions and delegate everything else, including, increasingly, to AI systems.

The question is not whether you trust AI. It is whether you have a clear enough model of your own thinking to let it help.

Read the full piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ?oc=5

post3.text:
Harvard Business Review is telling executives that AI demands new performance metrics. We have been thinking about this problem too.

Chapter 8 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is called The Third Monitor Theory. Written by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain, it tackles exactly how leaders at scaling companies should think about AI, not as a software decision, but as a strategic one that reshapes how your whole organization operates and measures itself.

The chapter opens with a simple but uncomfortable observation: most executives are reacting to AI rather than leading with it. There is a specific framework inside for how to get ahead of that gap before it costs you at scale.

We will leave the rest for you to read.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: AI is not slowing down for your org chart. Executives at companies scaling past $50M are already discovering that their performance metrics, their leadership bandwidth, and their decision-making models were built for a world that no longer exists. Here is what the shift actually looks like inside a growing business, and what to do before it catches you off guard.
posts.email_subject: Your KPIs are already out of date

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

post1.text:
The most dangerous leaders at the $50M+ level are not the ones who doubt themselves. They are the ones who stopped doubting themselves too early.

Imposter syndrome gets treated like a problem to solve. But Forbes Australia recently examined why self-doubt tends to track alongside high achievement, not against it. That finding should reframe how scaling executives think about their own discomfort.

Here is what we see consistently working with growth-stage companies:

1. Leaders who feel some version of imposter syndrome are often the ones still asking hard questions about the business. That instinct is an asset, not a liability.

2. The real risk is suppressing that doubt instead of channeling it. Leaders who perform confidence they do not feel make worse strategic decisions than those who sit with the discomfort long enough to learn from it.

3. At scale, psychological self-awareness is not a soft skill. It is a competitive variable that shows up in hiring, culture, and execution.

So here is the question worth sitting with: Are you working through self-doubt, or are you just getting better at hiding it?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company challenges leaders to stop using the term imposter syndrome altogether, arguing the label itself can do more harm than good by pathologizing a normal human response to new levels of responsibility.

At Autobahn, we think this distinction matters enormously for companies scaling past $50M. When leadership teams frame internal doubt as a disorder to overcome, they often rush past the signal it is sending. That signal frequently points to real gaps: in team structure, decision-making authority, or strategic clarity.

Leaders who scale successfully do not eliminate self-doubt. They build systems and teams that make the doubt productive instead of paralyzing.

If this framing resonates, read the full piece and ask yourself how your leadership culture handles uncertainty at the top.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidkFVX3lxTE9PRnBrU1NWWm1BeEl3dk5qVjFRc0M0T2ctZW9fbjdWd2NmdFlUZVVNeW9JV0VTWkktVmp0MlVjbFVEMXEweWZBS1d6bFBSMVNoNFVfWm5WaFo0aG1PT1RJN0p2Ylc0SkRONnBBcDYzZmNEUUJsRFE

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and it tackles something most business books skip entirely: the psychological experience of leading a company through serious growth.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, the chapter does not offer a quick fix for imposter syndrome. Instead, it surfaces a pattern we see in leaders scaling from $50M and beyond: the very traits that drove early success often become sources of internal conflict at the next level.

One insight that stands out: the leaders who scale most effectively are not the ones who feel most certain. They are the ones who have learned to lead with clarity even when certainty is not available.

That distinction is harder to build than most leadership frameworks acknowledge. Chapter 9 gets into exactly how.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader who has grown a company past $50M has felt it: the quiet, persistent suspicion that the results are outpacing the person delivering them. Most treat that feeling as a weakness to manage. The leaders who scale furthest treat it as data. Here is what separates the two groups, and why it matters more than any operational framework you will deploy this year.
posts.email_subject: The doubt that follows high achievers

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition - High Point University
articles.snippet: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition  High Point University

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
Most founders treat life planning like something they will get to after the business is figured out. That is a trap that costs them everything they built the business for.

Morgan Stanley recently mapped out what happens to entrepreneurs after a liquidity event. The pattern is consistent: founders who never defined what they wanted their life to look like are lost once the transaction closes. The exit was the goal. Not the life after it.

Here is what leaders scaling past $50M need to hear now, not later:

1. Your business strategy and your life strategy need to be built in parallel, not sequentially. One informs the other.

2. Time is the resource you cannot repurchase. Revenue can recover. Relationships and health rarely do.

3. The executives who scale best are the ones who know what they are scaling toward, personally, not just financially.

You can optimize a business for a decade and still arrive somewhere you never wanted to be.

So ask yourself: what is this company actually in service of in your life?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, drawing on Morgan Stanley research, lays out what life looks like for entrepreneurs after selling their businesses. The findings are worth sitting with. Many founders arrive at the finish line without a plan for what comes next and spend years recalibrating.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern well before the exit. Leaders scaling from $50M to $500M are so focused on the operational climb that they stop asking whether the climb is pointed in the right direction.

The companies that scale with the least collateral damage are the ones where leadership has intentionally designed their work around their life, not the other way around.

If you are in a growth stage and have not pressure-tested your personal priorities against your business roadmap, now is the right time to start.

Read the full Fortune piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
A Fortune piece on life after selling a business raises a question we think every scaling leader should answer much earlier: what are you actually building this for?

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled Coming Up for Air, takes that question seriously. Authored by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, this chapter addresses something most business books skip entirely: the intentional design of your life alongside your business, not after it.

One insight the chapter surfaces is that leaders who wait until the business is stable to think about their personal priorities often find the business has already made those decisions for them.

Growth without that clarity is just speed without a destination.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling leaders have a five-year plan for their business and no plan at all for their life. By the time the company hits $200M, the calendar is full and the important stuff has been quietly crowded out. Here is why life planning is not a soft topic, it is a business-critical discipline that your growth strategy cannot afford to ignore.
posts.email_subject: You planned the business. Did you plan your life?
